text: A 67-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe chest pain radiating to his back. BP is 190/100 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Aortic dissection: sudden tearing pain radiating to BACK + hypertension + BP difference between arms. Dx: CT angiography. Do NOT anticoagulate.

text: A 4-year-old child has a barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and mild respiratory distress worse at night. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Croup (parainfluenza virus): barking seal-like cough + stridor + hoarseness. Tx: Dexamethasone + racemic epinephrine if severe.

text: A 22-year-old male has sudden severe testicular pain. Testicle is high-riding and cremasteric reflex is absent. Next best step?
rationale: Testicular torsion = surgical emergency. Do NOT delay for imaging. 6-hour window for salvage. Absent cremasteric reflex = most reliable sign.

text: A patient has irregularly irregular pulse, palpitations, and fatigue. ECG shows no identifiable P waves. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves. Most feared complication = embolic stroke. Tx: rate control + anticoagulation (DOAC preferred).

text: A 50-year-old man has sudden severe headache he describes as the worst headache of his life while exercising. Neuro exam normal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise. Dx: CT head first → if negative → LP for xanthochromia. Cause: ruptured berry aneurysm.

text: A 45-year-old obese woman has RUQ pain after eating a fatty meal, fever, and positive Murphy sign. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute cholecystitis: RUQ pain + fever + Murphy sign + fatty meal trigger. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: IV fluids + antibiotics + cholecystectomy.

text: A 5-year-old has high fever, drooling, dysphagia, tripod position, and looks toxic. Inspiratory stridor present. Diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis: drooling + dysphagia + tripod + toxic = AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Secure airway FIRST. Do NOT examine throat. IV ceftriaxone after airway secured.

text: A 35-year-old man has sudden severe flank pain radiating to the groin, hematuria, and colicky nausea. Diagnosis?
rationale: Renal colic: colicky flank pain radiating to groin + hematuria. Dx: CT KUB. Tx: NSAIDs + IV fluids + tamsulosin. <5mm: 90% pass spontaneously.

text: A 4-week-old male infant has projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feeding. Appears hungry after vomiting. Olive-shaped mass in epigastrium. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pyloric stenosis: projectile non-bilious vomiting at 2-6 weeks + olive mass + hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. First-born males. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

text: A 54-year-old man has epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. Serum lipase is 3x upper limit of normal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back + lipase >3x ULN. Causes: Gallstones #1, Alcohol #2. Tx: aggressive IV fluids, NPO, analgesia.

text: A 68-year-old man has painless progressive jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and 12 kg weight loss over 3 months. Diagnosis?
rationale: Painless jaundice + weight loss in elderly = pancreatic head cancer. Courvoisier sign: palpable non-tender gallbladder. Dx: CT abdomen. CA 19-9 elevated.

text: A 40-year-old woman has symmetric joint pain/swelling in MCP and PIP joints, morning stiffness 90 minutes, positive RF and anti-CCP. Diagnosis?
rationale: RA: symmetric MCP/PIP (not DIP) + morning stiffness >1h + RF/anti-CCP positive. Tx: Methotrexate (DMARD) first-line. Anti-CCP is most specific.

text: A 55-year-old man has sudden severe pain, redness, and swelling of the first MTP joint overnight. Uric acid elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale: Gout: podagra (first MTP joint) + hyperuricemia + negatively birefringent needle crystals. Acute Tx: NSAIDs or colchicine. Prevention: allopurinol.

text: A 30-year-old woman has weight loss, palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, diarrhea. Exam shows exophthalmos and diffuse goiter. Diagnosis?
rationale: Graves disease: most common hyperthyroidism. Exophthalmos = pathognomonic. Low TSH + high T3/T4. Tx: Methimazole first-line.

text: A 45-year-old man has central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, purple abdominal striae, and hypertension. Diagnosis?
rationale: Cushing syndrome: excess cortisol. Most common cause: exogenous steroids. Endogenous: ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease).

text: A 35-year-old woman has persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, and fatigue for 6 weeks. She denies suicidal ideation. First-line treatment?
rationale: MDD first-line: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine). Allow 4-6 weeks for response. Treat minimum 6-9 months after remission.

text: A 20-year-old man has 8-month history of auditory hallucinations, believing he is controlled by aliens, social withdrawal, and flat affect. Diagnosis?
rationale: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) + negative symptoms (flat affect, withdrawal) >6 months. Tx: atypical antipsychotics. Clozapine for treatment-resistant.

text: A 9-month-old infant has episodic severe crampy abdominal pain, draws up legs, vomiting, and currant jelly stool. Abdomen distended. Diagnosis?
rationale: Intussusception: colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stool. Most common 6mo-3yr. Dx: Ultrasound (target sign). Tx: air enema reduction; surgery if failed.

text: A 60-year-old man has black tarry stools and lightheadedness. He takes ibuprofen daily. Melena indicates bleeding from which location?
rationale: Melena = digested blood = upper GI source above ligament of Treitz. NSAID use → peptic ulcer = #1 cause. Dx: urgent endoscopy. Tx: PPI + endoscopic hemostasis.

text: A 72-year-old woman has sudden right-sided weakness, facial droop, and inability to speak for 2 hours. Most appropriate first investigation?
rationale: Acute stroke: CT head NON-CONTRAST first to exclude hemorrhage before tPA. MRI more sensitive but slower. tPA window: 4.5 hours. Door-to-needle <60 min.

text: A 75-year-old woman has progressive loss of central vision and cannot read or recognize faces. Peripheral vision intact. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AMD: central vision loss (macula) in elderly. Dry more common. Wet (neovascular): anti-VEGF injections. Risk factors: age, smoking, family history.

text: A 60-year-old man has gradual painless loss of peripheral tunnel vision. IOP is elevated. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Glaucoma: increased IOP → optic nerve damage → peripheral tunnel vision loss. Open-angle most common. Tx: prostaglandin eye drops (latanoprost).

text: A 3-year-old child has fever >5 days, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, rash, and swollen hands/feet. Diagnosis?
rationale: Kawasaki disease: fever >5 days + 4/5 CRASH criteria. Tx: IVIG + aspirin. Most feared complication: coronary artery aneurysm. Screen with echocardiography.

text: A 32-week pregnant woman has painful vaginal bleeding and a rigid tender uterus. Fetal heart rate is abnormal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Placental abruption: PAINFUL bleeding + rigid uterus + fetal distress. Risk: HTN, trauma, cocaine. Tx: immediate delivery if fetal distress.

text: A 30-week pregnant woman has painless bright red vaginal bleeding. No contractions or trauma. Diagnosis?
rationale: Placenta previa: PAINLESS bright red bleeding. Placenta covers cervical os. Do NOT do digital exam. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: C-section if severe or at term.

text: A 64-year-old man has progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, bilateral lung crackles, and pitting leg edema. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: CHF: dyspnea + orthopnea + crackles + edema + elevated BNP. Tx: furosemide + ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker.

text: Which medication reduces mortality in systolic heart failure?
rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality by decreasing afterload and preventing remodeling. Diuretics relieve symptoms only. Also: beta-blockers and spironolactone reduce mortality.

text: Best imaging for suspected aortic dissection in a hemodynamically stable patient?
rationale: CT angiography: fast, highly sensitive (98%), gold standard for aortic dissection. CXR shows widened mediastinum but is NOT diagnostic.

text: A 70-year-old smoker has hemoptysis, weight loss, and a 3 cm spiculated lung mass on CXR. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Lung cancer: smoker + hemoptysis + weight loss + spiculated mass. Adenocarcinoma is #1 type overall. Small cell: central, highly aggressive, paraneoplastic syndromes.

text: Which type of lung cancer is most common overall?
rationale: Adenocarcinoma: #1 lung cancer overall, peripheral location, found in smokers and non-smokers. Squamous cell = central, cavitating. Small cell = most aggressive, paraneoplastic.

text: Recommended lung cancer screening for high-risk patients (age 50-80, heavy smokers)?
rationale: Annual low-dose CT: age 50-80 with ≥20 pack-year history, currently smoking or quit <15 years. CXR is NOT adequate for screening.

text: A 25-year-old man has RLQ pain migrating from periumbilical region, fever 38.3°C, and rebound at McBurney point. WBC 13,000. Diagnosis?
rationale: Appendicitis: periumbilical pain → RLQ + rebound at McBurney point + fever + leukocytosis. Tx: laparoscopic appendectomy.

text: Which physical sign is most classic for acute appendicitis?
rationale: McBurney point: 1/3 from ASIS to umbilicus. Murphy sign = cholecystitis. Cullen/Grey Turner = hemorrhagic pancreatitis. Rovsing sign also supports appendicitis.

text: A 60-year-old woman has RUQ pain, fever 39°C, and jaundice (Charcot triad). Reynolds pentad adds hypotension and confusion. Diagnosis?
rationale: Reynolds pentad = Charcot triad + hypotension + AMS = severe ascending cholangitis. Life-threatening. Tx: IV antibiotics + urgent ERCP for biliary decompression.

text: A 72-year-old man has sudden right-sided weakness. What percentage of strokes are ischemic?
rationale: 85% of strokes are ischemic. 15% hemorrhagic. CT head distinguishes: ischemic = normal early; hemorrhagic = hyperdense white blood immediately visible.

text: What is the most important modifiable risk factor for stroke?
rationale: Hypertension = #1 modifiable risk factor for stroke. Controlling BP reduces stroke risk by 40%. Other risks: AF, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia.

text: A patient has polyuria, polydipsia, and random blood glucose of 14 mmol/L with symptoms. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: DM: random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L with symptoms = diagnostic. Or: fasting ≥7.0, HbA1c ≥6.5%, 2h OGTT ≥11.1.

text: Which test is used to both diagnose AND monitor long-term diabetes control?
rationale: HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes. Reflects 3-month average glucose. Treatment target: <7%. Cannot use if hemoglobinopathy or hemolytic anemia.

text: First-line pharmacotherapy for newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes without cardiovascular disease?
rationale: Metformin: first-line T2DM. No hypoglycemia, weight neutral, cheap. With CVD or CKD: add SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA for organ protection.

text: A 20-year-old has sore throat, fever, and white tonsillar exudate. No cough. Rapid strep test positive. Most likely organism?
rationale: Group A Strep: Centor criteria = exudate + fever + anterior nodes + NO cough. Tx: penicillin or amoxicillin x10 days to prevent rheumatic fever.

text: First-line antibiotic for Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis?
rationale: Penicillin V x10 days = gold standard. Goal: prevent acute rheumatic fever. Amoxicillin acceptable. Azithromycin if penicillin-allergic.

text: A 35-year-old immigrant has 3-week cough, night sweats, and 8 kg weight loss. CXR shows upper lobe cavitary lesion. Diagnosis?
rationale: TB: cough >3 weeks + constitutional symptoms + upper lobe cavity. Dx: sputum AFB x3 + culture. Tx: RIPE x2 months then RI x4 months.

text: A 4-year-old child has barking cough and mild stridor. First-line treatment for croup?
rationale: Croup: oral dexamethasone for ALL severity levels. Moderate-severe: add racemic epinephrine nebulization. Epiglottitis: drooling + toxic appearance = different emergency.

text: Most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants under 2 years?
rationale: RSV = #1 cause of bronchiolitis. Treatment: supportive (hydration + O₂). Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk premature infants.

text: Which neurotransmitter deficiency is primarily implicated in major depressive disorder?
rationale: Serotonin deficiency = monoamine hypothesis of depression. SSRIs block serotonin reuptake. Also: norepinephrine and dopamine involved.

text: A 28-year-old has recurrent panic attacks with palpitations and fear of dying. Cardiac workup normal. First-line LONG-TERM treatment?
rationale: Panic disorder: SSRIs + CBT long-term. Benzodiazepines SHORT-TERM acute attacks only (dependence risk). Beta-blockers for performance anxiety.

text: A patient in ER has severe acute panic attack with trembling and palpitations. Fastest-acting treatment?
rationale: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam) = fastest relief of acute panic (minutes). For long-term prevention: SSRI. Avoid long-term benzodiazepines (tolerance + dependence).

text: A 52-year-old woman smokes 1 pack/day for 30 years. Which lung cancer screening is recommended?
rationale: Annual low-dose CT: age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, currently smoking or quit <15 years. Reduces lung cancer mortality ~20%. CXR NOT adequate for screening.

text: A competent 55-year-old Jehovah's Witness refuses a blood transfusion needed for surgery. He understands he may die. Most appropriate action?
rationale: Competent adult autonomy: right to refuse ANY treatment including life-saving. Document capacity assessment. Explore alternatives (cell salvage, erythropoietin). Family cannot override capable adult.

text: A physician prescribes the wrong dose of medication. Patient takes it but suffers no harm. What is the physician's obligation?
options: No disclosure needed since no harm occurred
rationale: Canadian ethics: full disclosure of adverse events AND near-misses is mandatory even if no harm. Apology is not legal admission of liability (Ontario Apology Act). Transparency builds trust.
